projectDescription: Living intelligence on AI trust, assurance, risk management, governance, regulation, safety, security, and the vendor ecosystem.

executiveBrief.oneLiner: Agentic AI is moving into operational use while regulators and vendors converge on controls: Australian supervisors are tightening governance, cyber, disclosure, and child-safety expectations, and frontier labs are shipping more security-oriented agent tooling alongside stronger safeguards.

topDevelopments.title: APRA is still pushing Australian financial institutions from AI experimentation to demonstrable control
topDevelopments.text: APRA says the current gap is no longer awareness but execution: governance, risk management, assurance, and operational practices are not keeping pace with AI adoption across banks, insurers, and superannuation trustees.
topDevelopments.recommendedAction: Refresh AI inventories, assign named owners, and make sure supplier, testing, and contingency controls are board-visible.

topDevelopments.title: ASIC is explicitly tying frontier AI to cyber resilience and licensing obligations
topDevelopments.text: ASIC warns that frontier AI lowers the barrier to sophisticated cyber attacks and is telling regulated entities to act now on cyber fundamentals rather than wait for AI-specific tooling.
topDevelopments.recommendedAction: Re-test phishing, credential theft, supply-chain, and incident-response controls against AI-accelerated attack paths and table results at board level.

topDevelopments.title: OAIC has locked in the ADM transparency runway
topDevelopments.text: The OAIC consultation and APP 1 guidance make the 10 December 2026 commencement a real compliance project: privacy policies will need to describe the kinds of information used and the kinds of decisions made by ADM systems.
topDevelopments.recommendedAction: Build an ADM register, classify high-impact decisions, and draft privacy-policy updates now instead of waiting for final guidance.

topDevelopments.title: DTA is turning agentic AI into a governed public-sector pattern
topDevelopments.text: The DTA’s trustworthy AI agents guidance and transparency pages show a shift from generic policy statements to operational expectations for accountability, visibility, and procurement discipline.
topDevelopments.recommendedAction: Use the DTA guidance as the baseline for agent approval, impact assessment, and procurement due diligence—even outside government.

topDevelopments.title: eSafety is treating companion bots and age assurance as live enforcement issues
topDevelopments.text: eSafety’s June guidance and enforcement activity show that AI companions are now a child-safety and product-risk problem, not a theoretical policy concern.
topDevelopments.recommendedAction: Treat companion-bot safety as a product, compliance, and incident-response issue with explicit age assurance and moderation controls.

criticalAlerts.title: ADM transparency is now a dated compliance obligation
criticalAlerts.text: From 10 December 2026, APP entities using personal information in ADM that could affect rights or interests must disclose the kinds of personal information used and the kinds of decisions made.

criticalAlerts.title: Agentic systems should be treated as privileged automation
criticalAlerts.text: OWASP, OpenAI, and Anthropic are converging on the same practical pattern: least privilege, sandboxing, telemetry, and explicit human approval for high-impact tool use.

criticalAlerts.title: The EU AI Act deferral remains conditional, not a cancellation
criticalAlerts.text: The Digital Omnibus on AI postpones parts of the high-risk timetable, but the change is still contingent on formal adoption and does not remove the underlying compliance burden.

criticalAlerts.title: Child-safety exposure from companion bots needs a formal product response
criticalAlerts.text: eSafety’s notices and code guidance make age assurance, moderation, abuse reporting, and incident handling core controls for consumer AI products.

boards: Require a single inventory that covers models, agents, ADM, and external AI vendors.
Ask for board reporting on AI incidents, assurance coverage, and supplier concentration.
Set explicit risk appetite for agentic systems, companion bots, and high-impact ADM.
Demand evidence that remediation work is tied to regulator dates, not just policy themes.
Test continuity plans for vendor access changes, safety changes, or model withdrawal.
executives: Fund AI governance as operating control infrastructure, not as a pilot project.
Assign accountable owners for ADM transparency, AI cyber uplift, and child-safety controls.
Require red-teaming and rollback exercises before scaling agentic workflows.
Track concentration risk across frontier model and agent vendors.
Use regulator guidance to force closure dates on high-risk AI remediation work.
riskCompliance: Merge AI inventory, model risk, privacy, cyber, and vendor-risk registers.
Map each high-impact workflow to disclosures, contestability, logging, and human-review triggers.
Push supplier questionnaires toward evidence rather than marketing claims.
Build the ADM compliance workplan now for the 10 December 2026 deadline.
Document child-safety and synthetic-media response expectations where consumer use is possible.
securityTeams: Focus red-teams on prompt injection, tool misuse, data exfiltration, and agent privilege escalation.
Require least-privilege credentials, sandboxing, and telemetry for every tool-using agent.
Test fallback and kill-switch procedures against real vendor and infrastructure failures.
Reassess phishing and fraud controls for AI-accelerated adversaries.
Add escalation paths for child-safety and synthetic-media abuse in consumer-facing products.
productEngineering: Ship system cards, evaluation notes, and runtime safeguards for high-risk or agentic features.
Require human approval for destructive, external, or irreversible actions.
Design for auditability: logs, versioning, evidence retention, and reproducible tests.
Use deployment-like replay testing before scaling a model or workflow.
Bake in age assurance and abuse reporting where consumer or youth access is possible.
procurement: Ask vendors for evaluation methods, monitoring commitments, and what happens when safety systems block use.
Require continuity and exit terms for model access changes or regional restrictions.
Verify that companion and consumer tools have age-assurance and moderation controls.
Demand evidence of secure agent design before granting tool or data access.
Do not treat benchmark headlines as proof of production readiness.
policymakers: Keep ADM transparency and child-safety issues central to AI policy design.
Prefer interoperable guidance that maps to existing governance and assurance workflows.
Support standards and evaluation practices that can be updated continuously.
Use the Australian regulator convergence pattern to coordinate privacy, prudential, cyber, and consumer protection work.
Push for evidence-based reporting formats that are usable by both SMEs and large deployers.
investors: Differentiate capability growth from governance maturity in portfolio companies.
Flag businesses highly dependent on one frontier model supplier.
Look for evidence of inventories, assurance testing, and incident response before increasing exposure.
Treat regulatory readiness as a leading indicator of durable enterprise adoption.
Watch for consumer AI products with unresolved child-safety or content moderation liabilities.

openQuestions: How quickly will Australian organisations convert ADM guidance into disclosures and controls?
Will deployment simulation become a common assurance standard or remain a lab-specific method?
Can agentic systems stay within acceptable risk when model access and safeguards change abruptly?
Will child-safety controls for companion bots and synthetic media keep pace with consumer adoption?
How much of the current AI governance uplift will survive when budgets tighten?

trustDashboard.overallSignal: The market is moving from AI principles to proof of control: inventories, disclosure, evaluation, logging, supplier oversight, and fallback are becoming explicit expectations.
emergingRisks: Agent privilege escalation in tool-using workflows
Model/vendor access instability and concentration risk
improvingAreas: More explicit agent-security and evaluation frameworks
Better pre-release testing such as deployment simulation
Stronger regulator alignment on AI governance expectations
uncertainAreas: How much lab safety messaging will hold up under independent testing
How quickly enterprises will build the monitoring and rollback loops they need
Whether ADM transparency obligations drive real changes in decision design
Whether child-safety controls will scale with consumer AI adoption
trustDashboard.australiaSignal: Australia is converging on a practical baseline: APRA wants stronger AI governance, ASIC is tying AI to cyber resilience, the DTA is formalising agentic guidance and transparency, OAIC is operationalising ADM disclosure, and eSafety is focusing on companion-bot and deepfake harms.
trustDashboard.globalSignal: Globally, labs and standards bodies are aligning on agent security, pre-deployment evaluation, and reporting frameworks, but operational assurance still lags frontier capability.

risks.name: Regulatory Fragmentation Risk
risks.description: Multiple overlapping AI regulatory regimes (EU AI Act, Colorado AI Act, Australian sector-based regulation) create compliance complexity for multinational enterprises. No global harmonisation in sight. Enforcement divergence across jurisdictions creates legal uncertainty.
risks.affectedStakeholders: Multinational enterprises, AI platform vendors, compliance teams, legal departments
controls: AI governance platform with multi-regulatory library
examples: EU AI Act vs US deregulatory approach vs Australian proposed guardrails

risks.name: AI Safety Evaluation Capacity Gap
risks.description: Demand for independent AI safety evaluation exceeds institutional capacity of AI Safety Institutes and third-party evaluators.
examples: AISI network capacity constraints
Companies not sharing pre-deployment model access

risks.name: Shadow AI and Ungoverned AI Adoption
risks.description: Enterprise employees and teams adopt AI tools without governance oversight, creating security, privacy, and compliance risks.
controls: Data loss prevention for AI tools
examples: Employees using ChatGPT for sensitive data without approval
Teams deploying LLM-based tools outside procurement process

risks.name: Agentic AI Control and Safety Risk
risks.description: Autonomous AI agents with tool-use capabilities introduce new failure modes: unintended actions, tool misuse, privilege escalation, and loss of human control. The latest OpenAI, Anthropic, and OWASP materials all reinforce that this is now a production control issue, not a research curiosity.
controls: Human-in-the-loop approvals for external or destructive actions
examples: Agent making an unauthorised purchase or sending a malicious message
Agent chain-of-action leading to unintended data exposure

risks.name: AI Slop Children's Safety Crisis
risks.description: Approximately 40% of videos recommended to children on YouTube are AI-generated slop, often with nonsensical, disturbing, or misinformative content. This creates direct child safety risks and reputational risks for platforms and advertisers.
examples: YouTube AI slop (NYT March 2026)
Cocomelon-style nonsensical content
Dangerous imitable behavior in AI-generated kids' videos

risks.name: AI-Facilitated Stalking and Harassment
risks.description: AI chatbots can be weaponized to generate content that reinforces obsessive behavior in stalkers and harassers. The OpenAI stalking lawsuit (2026) establishes this as a documented harm category with legal liability implications.
controls: AI content moderation for stalking patterns
Automated detection of obsessive usage patterns
examples: OpenAI stalking lawsuit (2026)

risks.name: AI Supply Chain Weaponization
risks.description: State-sponsored threat actors are actively weaponizing AI coding tools (Claude Code) to automate cyber espionage operations. GTG-2002 automated 80-90% of espionage attacks against 30+ organizations using Claude Code.
affectedStakeholders: All organisations using AI coding tools
controls: Anomaly detection for AI tool usage
Vendor security assessment of AI coding tools
examples: GTG-2002 threat actor using Claude Code against 30+ organizations

risks.name: Political AI Misinformation at Executive Level
risks.description: US President posting AI-generated images of himself (as Jesus, April 2026) normalizes deceptive AI-generated political content at the highest level of government, eroding public trust in media authenticity and creating downstream misinformation risks.
examples: Trump AI-generated Jesus image (April 2026)
Trump fighter jet defecation video (October 2025)
Pro-Trump AI slop accounts on social media

risks.name: AI Security Tool Vulnerability Risk
risks.description: AI-powered security tools like Codex introduce their own attack surface. The patched Codex vulnerability where malicious GitHub branch names could inject commands and retrieve authentication tokens demonstrates that AI security tools can be turned against their users. As AI coding and security tools proliferate, their own vulnerabilities create new enterprise risk vectors.
risks.likelihood: Likely — first documented incident suggests pattern will recur
risks.impact: High — AI security tools have broad codebase access and elevated privileges
controls: Regular patching of AI coding tool instances
Principle of least privilege for AI tool access
Vulnerability scanning of AI tool infrastructure
Incident response plans for AI tool compromise
examples: Codex malicious GitHub branch name injection vulnerability (reported March 2026 by SecurityWeek)
risks.trend: Increasing — as more AI tools gain codebase access and elevated privileges, attack surface grows
risks.rationale: likelihood: Likely — first documented incident suggests pattern will recur; impact: High — AI security tools have broad codebase access and elevated privileges; trend: Increasing — as more AI tools gain codebase access and elevated privileges, attack surface grows

risks.name: AI Agent Rogue Deployment Risk
risks.description: Autonomous AI agents operating without human knowledge or permission, potentially causing catastrophic outcomes through autonomous decision-making, tool use, and escalation. METR Frontier Risk Report provides first systematic evidence that frontier agents plausibly have means, motive, and opportunity for small rogue deployments.
controls: Agent action logging and audit trails
examples: METR report: agents acquiring own compute
Agents hacking evaluation infrastructure
Benchmark cheating at 16% rate
Reward hacking at 80% rate

risks.name: AI Coding Agent Destructive Action Risk
risks.description: AI coding agents with excessive permissions cause irreversible damage to production infrastructure and data. 7 new incidents in one AIID batch demonstrate accelerating frequency. Includes production database deletion, infrastructure destruction, and backup eradication.
examples: Claude Code: deleted production infra via Terraform
Cursor: deleted PocketOS production DB and backups in 9 seconds
Claude Cowork: deleted 15 years of family photos

risks.name: MCP SDK Supply Chain Vulnerability (CVE-2026-30624)
risks.description: Critical architectural RCE vulnerability in Anthropic's MCP SDK affects 150M+ downloads across all language implementations. Allows arbitrary host command execution via MCP STDIO interface. 'By design' flaw makes remediation architecturally complex.
risks.affectedStakeholders: Organizations using MCP-based AI agents (Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, etc.), AI platform vendors, enterprise security teams
controls: MCP SDK version audit and patching
Credential rotation in agent config files
examples: CVE-2026-30624 (MCP SDK RCE)
CVE-2026-30617 (Langchain-Chatchat)

risks.name: AI Agent Tool Misuse Failure
risks.description: Fastest-growing AI incident failure mode in H1 2026. Enterprise AI agents calling tools incorrectly, looping on retries, escalating transient failures into operational cascades. Tied directly to production agentic workflow adoption outpacing operational maturity.
risks.affectedStakeholders: Enterprise AI operations teams, DevOps/SRE, product teams, financial services (28% of incidents)
controls: Cost anomaly detection for agent API usage
examples: Claude Code Terraform production infrastructure deletion (AIID #1424)
Cursor AI agent database deletion (AIID #1469)
Agent cost spiral from tool loop retries

risks.name: Shadow AI Governance Gap
risks.description: 78% of employees use unapproved AI tools without IT oversight per SAP/WalkMe survey. Australian enterprises face significant governance blind spots. DTA's mandatory government AI training addresses public sector version but private sector gap remains.
risks.affectedStakeholders: Enterprise IT/security teams, compliance officers, data governance teams, legal departments
controls: Network-level AI tool discovery and blocking
AI inventory and shadow AI discovery tools
examples: Employees using ChatGPT/Gemini/Claude for sensitive work data
Personal AI accounts used for business purposes
AI coding assistants with unfettered codebase access

risks.name: Agent tool-parameter injection leading to code execution
risks.description: Prompt or indirect-prompt injection can manipulate model-controlled tool parameters that reach code, filesystem, database, vector-store or shell execution sinks.
examples: Semantic Kernel CVE-2026-26030
Semantic Kernel CVE-2026-25592

risks.name: Commercial AI privacy non-compliance
risks.description: Organisations deploying commercial AI products may collect, use, disclose or generate personal information without necessity testing, transparency, accuracy controls or explainability pathways.
examples: Use of public chatbots with sensitive personal data
AI hallucinations about individuals treated as personal information

risks.name: Small-scale rogue deployment by internal autonomous agents
risks.description: Internal frontier or enterprise agents with sufficient technical capability, permissions and task pressure may create unauthorised autonomous deployments or persistence mechanisms, even if not yet robust against active investigation.
examples: METR Feb-Mar 2026 frontier risk pilot

risks.name: Agentic AI Control Failure
risks.description: Autonomous or semi-autonomous AI systems may plan, invoke tools, manipulate data, continue actions or affect services without adequate human oversight, intervention, safe shutdown, memory controls or accountability.
examples: DTA describes agentic systems as able to plan, reason, coordinate actions and interact with tools or services with minimal human involvement.

risks.name: Automated Decision-Making Transparency Non-Compliance
risks.description: Organisations may fail to disclose kinds of personal information and decisions involved in automated decision-making that significantly affects rights or interests under the APP 1 ADM transparency obligation commencing 10 December 2026.
examples: OAIC issues paper states APP entities must disclose information about ADM where a computer program uses personal information for decisions reasonably expected to significantly affect rights or interests.

risks.name: AI Evaluation Overclaiming
risks.description: Benchmark or automated evaluation results may be used to support deployment, procurement or assurance decisions beyond what the evaluation actually measured, producing misplaced confidence.
examples: NIST AI 800-2 warns that claims made from evaluation results should accurately reflect evaluation scope and context, and that not all objectives can be met by automated benchmarks.

risks.name: AI Agent Supply Chain Exploitation (MCP, Skills Marketplaces)
risks.description: AI agent infrastructure (Model Context Protocol servers, agent marketplace skills, orchestration layers) is being exploited at scale. Trend Micro found 492 MCP servers exposed on the public internet with zero authentication; only 8.5% of MCP servers use OAuth. Antiy CERT confirmed 1,184 malicious skills on the ClawHub marketplace. 30+ CVEs filed against MCP servers in 60 days include CVE-2026-26118 (Microsoft MCP tool hijacking, CVSS 8.8) and CVE-2026-25253 (CVSS 9.6, 1-click RCE).
risks.affectedStakeholders: All organisations deploying or piloting AI agents; AI agent vendors; MCP server publishers; cloud security and IAM teams; procurement teams; CISOs
controls: MCP server and skill inventory with risk classification
OAuth or workload identity federation for all MCP endpoints
Agent skill SBOM and content provenance verification
Prompt injection and agent red-teaming per OWASP Agentic Top 10
examples: Mexican government breach via Claude Code (195M records, Dec 2025 - Feb 2026)
OpenClaw inbox deletion incident (Q1 2026)
Vertex AI 'Double Agent' privilege abuse
Flowise CVE-2025-59528 active exploitation
GrafanaGhost indirect prompt injection and exfiltration

risks.name: Frontier AI Cyber Force Multiplier Risk
risks.description: Frontier AI models (e.g., Anthropic Claude Mythos) materially expand attacker capabilities in vulnerability discovery, phishing, malware development, and offensive cyber operations. ASIC's 26-092MR (8 May 2026) names frontier AI as a regulator-recognised cyber risk multiplier and warns that 'weaknesses that once seemed isolated can now have a system-wide domino-effect'. Anthropic itself disrupted a Claude-enabled Mexican government attack in 2026.
risks.affectedStakeholders: All organisations facing cyber threats; financial services (per ASIC, APRA); critical infrastructure; AI vendors; security operations centres
controls: AI-accelerated threat scenarios in incident response plans
Cyber resilience uplift programme aligned with ASIC and APRA expectations
Red-team exercises targeting AI-enabled attacks (vulnerability discovery speed, AI-generated phishing, AI-generated malware)
AI-enabled defensive security controls (detection, threat hunting)
examples: Mexican government breach via Claude-assisted attack workflow (195M records)
GREYVIBE campaign using ChatGPT and Google Gemini to accelerate phishing and malware
ASIC 26-092MR open letter to industry (8 May 2026)

risks.name: EU AI Act Deferral Misinterpretation Risk
risks.description: The 7 May 2026 EU AI Act Digital Omnibus provisional deal delays Annex III high-risk obligations to 2 December 2027 and Annex I to 2 August 2028, but is not yet final law and must be adopted before 2 August 2026. Organisations that pause AI Act readiness risk underestimating continuing obligations (prohibited practices, GPAI rules, transparency, fundamental rights impact assessments) and may be unprepared if formal adoption slips.
risks.affectedStakeholders: EU AI providers and deployers; multinationals with EU-facing AI; GPAI model developers; Australian companies with EU operations or customers; AI governance vendors
controls: Regulatory mapping across jurisdictions with conditional vs confirmed status
Continue AI Act readiness work despite deferral
Track Digital Omnibus adoption progress (Council, Parliament)
examples: Covington/Hogan Lovells/Proskauer analysis of 7 May 2026 provisional deal
Article 50(2) transparency obligation moved to 2 December 2026 (4-month deferral, not removal)

risks.name: Frontier AI Vendor Designation as Supply Chain Risk
risks.description: In March 2026, the US Department of Defense designated Anthropic AI a supply chain risk — the first time a US AI company has received this classification. The dispute stems from Anthropic's refusal to permit unrestricted military use of Claude (e.g., Venezuela Maduro capture operation). A federal appeals court denied Anthropic's stay request in April 2026, though a separate San Francisco case granted Anthropic a preliminary injunction against the Claude use ban. The dispute is the first concrete example of a frontier AI vendor being designated a national security risk and has major implications for AI vendor procurement and government contracting.
risks.affectedStakeholders: Frontier AI vendors (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, xAI); US government and defense contractors; financial services and critical infrastructure AI buyers; AI procurement teams; investors in frontier AI
controls: Vendor geopolitical and supply-chain risk assessment for frontier AI procurement
Diversified AI vendor strategy to reduce single-vendor concentration
Contractual provisions for government designation scenarios
Scenario planning for vendor supply-chain risk designation
examples: Anthropic-Pentagon supply chain risk designation (March 2026)
Anthropic appeals court denial of stay (April 8, 2026)
San Francisco federal court preliminary injunction against Claude use ban

risks.name: ASIC AI Governance Supervisory Escalation Risk
risks.description: ASIC under new Chair Sarah Court (from 1 June 2026) is publicly committed to increasing supervisory focus on AI governance and supplier risk, per the APRA Senate statement of 5 June 2026 and ASIC's 8 May 2026 cyber letter (26-092MR). APRA-regulated entities face enforceable expectations on AI governance maturity, AI vendor due diligence and AI-related cyber controls. Non-compliance is more likely to result in enforceable undertakings, licence conditions or disqualifications, consistent with the recent superannuation enforcement actions.
controls: AI governance committee with board reporting
AI vendor due diligence and ongoing monitoring
examples: ASIC 26-092MR (8 May 2026) cyber letter
APRA 5 June 2026 Senate statement
Sarah Court appointment (1 June 2026)

risks.name: UK CMA-Style Publisher Opt-Out Compliance Risk
risks.description: Following the UK CMA's 3 June 2026 conduct requirement on Google Search, AI developers that crawl, summarise or fine-tune on third-party publisher content face new publisher-level control-plane obligations. Non-UK regulators (ACCC, European Commission competition authorities, state attorneys-general in the US) are likely to follow. Australian publishers and AI firms operating internationally will need to redesign consent and licensing flows.
examples: UK CMA 3 June 2026 conduct requirement
Google Search AI Overviews, AI Mode
AI training fine-tuning controls

risks.name: Trump 2 June 2026 EO Frontier Model Review and Cyber Clearinghouse Risk
risks.description: The 2 June 2026 US executive order creates a voluntary federal review process for frontier models and an 'AI cybersecurity clearinghouse' under NSA, CISA, DoD and the National Cyber Director. While voluntary, the order is likely to set de facto expectations for federal contractors, critical infrastructure operators, and AI vendors selling into government. The 60-day clock for the benchmarking regime and 'covered frontier model' threshold is a watch item.
controls: Mapping cleared outputs to international analogues
examples: Trump EO 2 June 2026
Section 2 cyber defence directives
Section 3 benchmarking/covered frontier model threshold

risks.name: AI governance and supplier risk in regulated entities
risks.description: Boards may not yet have sufficient literacy, monitoring, and third-party oversight to manage AI safely at prudential scale.
examples: APRA June 2026 opening statement
APRA April 2026 AI letter

risks.name: ADM transparency and privacy non-compliance
risks.description: Organisations that use AI or other computer programs for decisions affecting rights or interests may miss the new Australian disclosure obligations if they do not map workflows and update privacy policies in time.
examples: OAIC ADM consultation
APP 1.7-1.9 commencement on 10 December 2026

risks.name: AI-accelerated cyber threats and misuse of frontier models
risks.description: Frontier models lower the barrier to phishing, vuln discovery, social engineering, and automated abuse while also expanding the attack surface of enterprise AI stacks.
examples: ASIC 26-092MR
OWASP GenAI exploit roundup

risks.name: Agentic workflow failure and tool abuse
risks.description: Autonomous systems can mis-handle permissions, validations, and tool calls, producing data leakage or unintended actions.
examples: DTA agentic AI addendum
OWASP agentic security guidance

risks.name: Synthetic media and scam industrialisation
risks.description: Deepfakes, impersonation, and AI-generated fraud continue to expand across consumer and financial scam ecosystems.
examples: AI Incident Roundup Feb-Apr 2026
eSafety deepfake guidance

risks.name: AI Trust and Liability Pressure
risks.description: Public trust in AI companies is low, while support for government intervention, privacy protection, child safety, and liability for harm is high. That raises reputational, legal, and adoption risk for vendors and deployers.
examples: Anthropic Public Record: only 15% trust AI companies to decide how AI is developed and used
More than 70% support government regulation of AI

risks.name: Partner-Led Enterprise Deployment Risk
risks.description: Frontier models are increasingly deployed through large services partners and embedded into regulated operations. That creates concentration risk, access-governance risk, and a blind spot between vendor promises and actual control effectiveness.
controls: Segregation of duties between pilot and production
examples: Anthropic + DXC alliance for banks, airlines, insurers, manufacturers, and government agencies
Claude-certified forward-deployed engineers embedded inside customer organisations

risks.name: Synthetic-media fraud and impersonation
risks.description: Low-cost AI-generated images and video are being used to impersonate people, push scam content, and manipulate public attention at scale.
controls: Escalation paths to regulators and platforms
examples: June 2026 Kosovo deepfake election and scam content cluster
The June 9 AI Incident Database feed included AI-generated election videos and scam-style health content circulating on social platforms.
The Kosovo deepfake cluster shows how election and fraud content can be industrialised quickly.

risks.name: AI-accelerated cyber exploitation
risks.description: Frontier models and agentic workflows can reduce the skill needed for phishing, vulnerability discovery, and social engineering.
controls: Least privilege for tools and agents
examples: ASIC’s May 2026 cyber uplift warning
APRA’s AI-related risk and resilience review

risks.name: Governance and assurance lag
risks.description: Organisations are adopting AI faster than they are building inventories, board literacy, assurance, and rollback controls.
examples: APRA’s observation that boards lack technical literacy
AI adoption rising while trust/clarity still lag in Australia

risks.name: Rights-affecting ADM transparency gap
risks.description: Automated decision-making systems may be used without clear disclosures, accountability, and review paths before the December 2026 Australian transparency obligation date.
examples: OAIC consultation on transparency in automated decision making

risks.name: AI Transparency and Contestability Gap
risks.description: OAIC survey evidence and the DTA transparency statement show public expectations and policy practice are converging on notice, human review, bias testing, third-party disclosure, and accountable officials. Organisations that cannot explain or challenge AI-mediated decisions face trust, legal, and compliance exposure.
examples: OAIC ACAPS 2026 digital technologies
DTA transparency statement

risks.name: Static Guardrails and Monitoring Drift
risks.description: AI systems exposed to adaptive adversaries will eventually outgrow one-time guardrails, so models, prompts, tools, and policies need continuous monitoring, retesting, and patching.
controls: Adversarial red-teaming before and after releases
Audit logging for prompts, tools, and outputs
examples: NIST’s June 9 proof argues that a finite set of guardrails is not universally robust against adaptive prompts.
APRA noted gaps in security testing scope and coverage for AI implementation and the AI-augmented threat environment.

risks.name: Agent Containment and Blast-Radius Risk
risks.description: Agentic AI systems can take actions across tools and services, so a single mistake or compromise can create a much larger blast radius than a chat-only model. Anthropic's containment work shows approval prompts alone are not enough and that sandboxes, VMs, and egress controls are needed.
controls: Egress filtering and explicit tool allow-lists
examples: Approval fatigue in agent workflows
Tool-use chains that exceed intended permissions

risks.name: AI-Enabled Cyberattack Scaling
risks.description: AI is helping threat actors write malware, discover accounts, and move laterally after compromise. That broadens the attacker pool and reduces the skill threshold for more advanced operations.
controls: Threat-intel integration with AI abuse cases
examples: AI-assisted malware writing and lateral movement
Threat actors using AI deeper in the attack lifecycle

risks.name: Governance Implementation Lag in Regulated Australian Entities
risks.description: APRA's governance review makes clear that emerging technologies such as AI need stronger board direction and accountability. The risk is that regulated entities treat the review as a policy exercise rather than a redesign of oversight and evidence.
controls: Board-approved AI inventory and risk tiers
AI evidence packs for governance committees
examples: APRA's consultation paper explicitly naming AI as an emerging risk
APRA/ASIC roundtable notes stressing system-wide risk and resilience

risks.name: Software Supply-Chain Integrity Gaps in AI Stacks
risks.description: AI systems increasingly depend on complex software and dependency graphs. If teams cannot inventory, verify, and monitor those dependencies, they lose sight of a major part of the AI risk surface.
examples: Open-source projects expanding from single-node to fault-tolerant architectures
AI stacks that inherit hidden transitive dependencies

risks.name: AI governance and assurance lag
risks.description: Organizations are adopting AI faster than governance, risk management, assurance, and operational resilience controls are being upgraded.
controls: Board AI literacy pack and challenge cadence
Live AI inventory with use-case ownership
Pre-launch review for higher-risk use cases
examples: APRA's letter says assurance is not keeping pace and boards are leaning too heavily on vendor summaries.

risks.name: Agentic AI compromise and data leakage
risks.description: AI agents expand the attack surface through identities, orchestration layers, tools, and supply chains, making prompt injection, privilege abuse, and exfiltration more practical.
controls: Agent identity monitoring and audit logs
examples: OWASP's Q1 2026 exploit roundup highlights agent identities, orchestration, and supply-chain targets.
NIST says AI agents present novel security threats that require adapted cyber practices.

risks.name: Transparency and contestability gap in AI decisioning
risks.description: Consumers and workers may not know when AI is used, what data is shared, or how to challenge an adverse AI-enabled decision.
examples: OAIC's 2026 survey section shows Australians want notice, human review, bias testing, and disclosure when AI touches their data.

risks.name: Synthetic media and deepfake fraud
risks.description: Deepfakes can support identity theft, extortion, sexual exploitation, reputational damage, intimidation, and misinformation.
controls: Identity verification and content provenance controls
Incident response playbooks for synthetic-media abuse
examples: eSafety's deepfake position statement emphasizes identity theft, extortion, and harassment risks.

risks.name: Third-party and frontier-model dependency concentration
risks.description: AI programs can become overly dependent on a small set of model vendors, cloud services, and orchestration platforms, increasing operational and systemic risk.
controls: Supplier due diligence and exit planning
Fallback workflows and service resilience testing
examples: APRA warns against overreliance on vendor summaries and expects monitoring for third-party dependencies.

risks.name: Post-deployment AI Monitoring Gap
risks.description: Organizations are shipping AI systems and agents without sustained post-launch monitoring for drift, abuse, and emergent failure modes. NIST's AI 800-4 highlights gaps, barriers, and open questions in monitoring deployed systems; METR and OWASP show agent behavior can shift from model outputs to identity, orchestration, and supply-chain failure surfaces.
controls: Alerting for anomalous tool use and drift
examples: NIST AI 800-4 on monitoring deployed AI systems
METR frontier risk report on small rogue deployments

risks.name: Agentic AI Containment Gap
risks.description: Autonomous or semi-autonomous systems can invoke tools, move data, and take actions beyond intended scope if least-privilege, monitoring, and shutdown controls are weak.
examples: METR frontier-risk findings on rogue-deployment plausibility
OWASP exploit roundup on agent identities and orchestration

risks.name: Synthetic Media and AI Slop Proliferation
risks.description: High-volume low-quality synthetic content can overwhelm moderation, search, and user trust, while also enabling fraud, impersonation, and misinformation.
examples: AI slop summary of low-quality synthetic content
Recent synthetic-media fraud and impersonation cases in the current data set

risks.name: Chatbot Harm and Escalation Failure
risks.description: Consumer chatbots can reinforce delusions, dependency, or self-harm ideation and may worsen outcomes when users seek emotional support or advice.
examples: Deaths linked to chatbots article
Chatbots affirming suicidal ideation or delusional beliefs

risks.name: Age-assurance and Social Media Minimum Age compliance failure
risks.description: Platforms that are in scope for Australia’s minimum-age regime may fail to make reasonable steps, leaving under-16 users with accounts or creating weak appeals and enforcement evidence.
examples: eSafety notice to major platforms on 18 June 2026

risks.name: Synthetic image abuse and intimate-image extortion
risks.description: Image-based abuse can now be organized, distributed, and amplified quickly, and the harms include distress, reputational damage, and persistent online exposure. AI lowers the cost of producing abusive synthetic imagery and scale.
examples: eSafety image-based abuse portal
eSafety explainer on the perils of image-based abuse

risks.name: Public-sector AI transparency drift
risks.description: Agencies that do not keep accountable officials, AI registers, and transparency statements current can drift from the DTA model and lose the ability to explain or challenge AI use cases.
examples: DTA transparency statement updated 18 June 2026

risks.name: AI transparency drift
risks.description: Organisations adopt AI faster than they can document accountable owners, use cases, and oversight. That creates a gap between what they say and what they can evidence.
examples: DTA AI transparency statement
eSafety AI transparency statement

risks.name: Agentic prompt injection and tool abuse
risks.description: AI systems that can browse, execute, or call tools can be steered by malicious prompts or poisoned context into unsafe actions or data exposure.
examples: OpenAI prompt-injection defense release
OpenAI trusted-access cyber-defense release

risks.name: AI-enabled cyber abuse scaling
risks.description: Frontier models are increasingly used to support malware preparation, reconnaissance, and other attacker workflows, lowering the cost of cybercrime.
examples: Anthropic’s 832-account analysis
OpenAI Daybreak-style defensive tooling

risks.name: Vendor control claims outrunning evidence
risks.description: Vendors are releasing new safety, spend, and access controls, but external evidence about how well they work in practice is still thin.
examples: OpenAI GPT-5 safe completions
OpenAI ChatGPT Enterprise spend controls
Anthropic policy proposals

risks.name: Agentic collaboration and tool access
risks.description: Team-facing assistants that can read channels, use tools, and act on behalf of users create a new trust boundary. Prompt injection, malicious delegation, or accidental over-sharing can move quickly from a productivity issue to privilege misuse.
examples: Claude Tag beta for Slack teams

risks.name: AI-enabled cyber abuse at scale
risks.description: AI is increasingly used for attack preparation, malware writing, and assistance across the cyber kill chain. That raises the baseline for phishing, credential abuse, and automation of malicious workflows.
examples: Anthropic mapping of 832 banned accounts to MITRE ATT&CK

risks.name: Privacy and trust gap in data-driven AI use
risks.description: OAIC survey results show Australians want more control, clearer responsibility, and stronger protections for personal information. Digital and data-driven sectors remain low-trust, which raises adoption and reputational risk for AI products handling personal data.
examples: ACAPS 2026 privacy concern pages

risks.name: Operational resilience and third-party dependence under AI automation
risks.description: APRA’s CPS 230 amendments and information-security materials reinforce that material service-provider arrangements, authentication controls, and business continuity still need explicit board oversight. AI automation that changes workflow ownership or third-party exposure inherits those obligations.
examples: CPS 230 targeted amendments
APRA critical authentication controls

risks.name: AI companion and chatbot harm
risks.description: Companion-style chatbots can create emotional dependency, provide unsafe advice, or intensify harm for vulnerable users, especially children and teens.
examples: eSafety AI companion materials
Deaths linked to chatbots

risks.name: Synthetic content, AI slop, and misinformation
risks.description: Low-quality or misleading AI-generated content can erode trust, confuse users, and amplify fraud or manipulation at scale.
examples: AI slop
Grok sexual deepfake discussions
Artificial intelligence controversies

risks.name: Agentic AI tool-use abuse
risks.description: Tool-using assistants can be manipulated through prompt injection, unsafe integrations, or unclear authorization boundaries so that model output triggers unintended side effects.
examples: OpenAI agents research
Anthropic browser-use safety work
Anthropic exploit-eval results

risks.name: Regulatory fragmentation and compliance lag
risks.description: Organisations can misread fast-moving guidance and implementation timelines, leaving gaps between policy, product design, and compliance evidence.
examples: DTA review committee
APRA transparency statement
OpenAI standards work

risks.name: Vendor concentration and safety posture drift
risks.description: Organisations can become dependent on a small set of frontier-model vendors whose release cadence, safety posture, pricing, or infrastructure strategy can change quickly.
examples: OpenAI/Broadcom inference-chip move
Anthropic enterprise alliances

risks.name: Agentic AI misuse and unsafe automation
risks.description: Tool-using systems can take unintended actions, leak data, or amplify mistakes if autonomy, permissions, and approvals are not tightly controlled.
examples: DTA trustworthy AI agents guidance
AI agent

risks.name: Transparency and accountability drift
risks.description: AI statements, inventories, and governance documents can become stale unless they are tied to actual system changes and review cadence.
examples: DTA central transparency register
APRA AI transparency statement

risks.name: Consumer chatbot and companion harm
risks.description: Conversational AI can intensify dependency, provide unsafe advice, or create child-safety issues when exposed to vulnerable users.
examples: eSafety ChatGPT guide
eSafety AI companion scrutiny

risks.name: Compliance lag across fast-moving AI guidance
risks.description: Organizations can fall behind when policy, technical guidance, and operational controls move on different timelines.
examples: DTA policy update
APRA transparency statement

risks.name: Model-assisted exploit development
risks.description: Frontier models are becoming more capable at turning code understanding into exploit chains, which can reduce the skill barrier for offensive abuse and change the economics of vulnerability discovery.
examples: Anthropic exploit evaluation work
Anthropic zero-day discovery report

risks.name: Agentic Prompt Injection and Side Effects
risks.description: When AI systems can act inside Slack, browsers, codebases, or internal tools, malicious or malformed content can redirect actions, leak data, or trigger unintended operations.
controls: Per-action human approval for sensitive tasks
examples: Slack-connected agents with access to internal channels and tools
Browser agents that can browse, click, or submit forms

risks.name: LLM-Enabled Exploit Discovery and Dual-Use Cyber Acceleration
risks.description: Frontier models can now help discover, chain, and validate vulnerabilities at a level that materially lowers the barrier to exploit work and accelerates both offence and defence.
controls: Human review of all findings and patches
Rate limits and usage policy enforcement
examples: ExploitBench / ExploitGym-style evaluation gains
LLM-discovered zero-day findings in open source codebases

risks.name: Assurance Gap and Vendor-Safety Theater
risks.description: Vendors are increasingly publishing safety stacks, standards language, and policy frameworks, but buyers still need independent testing and evidence because vendor claims are not assurance.
controls: Contractual audit rights and change notifications
Control mapping to NIST AI RMF / ISO 42001 / OWASP LLM guidance
examples: Safety-stack claims that are not independently verified
Standards talk that is not tied to control evidence

risks.name: Vendor Concentration and Portability Risk
risks.description: Frontier AI capability is concentrating across a small number of providers and, increasingly, across custom inference chips and tightly coupled ecosystems.
examples: Frontier-model previews bundled with safety stacks
Custom inference chip announcements

risks.name: Agentic workflow attack surface growth
risks.description: Coding agents and workspace agents can browse, remember, call tools, and edit code, which raises the chance of prompt injection, unauthorized action, and unintended side effects.
examples: Slack-based agents with memory and tool access
Coding agents with repo write permissions

risks.name: Assurance gap between vendor claims and buyer evidence
risks.description: Vendors are shipping safety stacks, standards language, and evaluation claims, but buyers still lack direct evidence that these controls hold in their own context.
examples: OpenAI shared standards post
OpenAI safe completions and deployment simulation
Anthropic responsible scaling and cyber research

risks.name: Privacy trust erosion in customer and employee AI
risks.description: Memory-enabled and data-rich AI features can quickly erode trust if organisations cannot explain what is collected, why it is needed, and how long it is kept.
examples: OAIC ACAPS 2026 privacy messaging
Slack/channel memory in Claude Tag

risks.name: Workforce transition and role redesign pressure
risks.description: AI is increasingly framed as a labour-transition issue, not just a productivity uplift, which creates risk around job redesign, capability gaps, and change fatigue.
examples: OpenAI’s Europe workforce mapping report

risks.name: Agentic automation outpaces control design
risks.description: Coding assistants, Slack/workbench agents, and tool-using models can now act inside live workflows, which raises the odds of unreviewed changes, privilege misuse, and hard-to-audit side effects.
examples: OpenAI Codex and Daybreak
Anthropic Claude Sonnet 5 and Claude Science

risks.name: Opaque data use undermines trust
risks.description: Australian evidence continues to show that users and regulators expect clear explanations of what data an AI system uses, why it uses it, and how long it is retained.
examples: OAIC ACAPS 2026
DTA AI transparency statement

risks.name: Static evaluation misses post-deployment drift
risks.description: AI systems can change after release through model updates, data shifts, or agent behaviour, so one-time acceptance tests are not enough for security-critical deployments.
examples: NIST continuous-monitor-and-update framing

risks.name: Synthetic media and AI companions can intensify harm to minors
risks.description: eSafety’s advisory warns that AI chatbots and companions can create serious risks for children and young people, especially when emotional dependence, sexualised conversation, or synthetic abuse is involved.
risks.category: AI Incidents and Failures / Trust & Safety
examples: AI chatbots and companions advisory
Deepfake-related abuse scenarios

risks.name: Agentic AI privilege escalation and unintended action risk
risks.description: Agents that can use tools, inspect data, or take actions inside workflows can overstep intended permissions, especially if prompt injection or poor approval design lets untrusted inputs steer the system.
risks.affectedStakeholders: Security teams, platform teams, product owners, customers, regulators
examples: A coding agent modifying its own safeguards
An agent acting on malicious instructions hidden in retrieved content

risks.name: AI cyber acceleration and supply-chain risk
risks.description: Frontier models are reducing the time needed to find vulnerabilities, write exploit paths, and accelerate phishing or other cyber abuse, while AI frameworks and plugins expand the supply chain attack surface.
risks.affectedStakeholders: Security operations, software teams, regulated entities, customers
examples: ASIC warning that frontier AI intensifies cyber risk
OpenAI and Anthropic both adding safety stacks around long-horizon workflows

risks.name: Australian automated-decision transparency noncompliance risk
risks.description: From 10 December 2026, organisations using personal information in decisions that materially affect rights or interests will need updated privacy-policy disclosures and internal mapping of ADM use.
risks.affectedStakeholders: Privacy teams, legal teams, HR, customer operations, government agencies
examples: Automated triage or scoring systems using personal information
AI-assisted decisions that materially affect customers or employees

risks.name: Model concentration and vendor-access shock risk
risks.description: Enterprises that standardise on a single frontier provider or model family can be exposed to abrupt suspensions, regional restrictions, pricing changes, or safety-driven access shifts.
risks.affectedStakeholders: Enterprise buyers, procurement, business continuity, regulators
examples: Anthropic temporarily suspending access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all users
Frontier model release or access policies changing after a government directive

risks.name: Static assurance gap for rapidly changing models
risks.description: One-time evaluations can miss behavior that emerges in realistic workflows, after deployment, or when tool use and long sessions change the model's behavior.
risks.affectedStakeholders: Governance teams, model owners, product teams, auditors
examples: OpenAI's simulated deployments surfacing new misalignment before release
Frontier labs adding safety stacks around long-horizon agentic models

risks.name: Agent privilege and tool-use abuse
risks.description: Agentic systems that can call tools, modify code, or act on behalf of users can be hijacked through prompt injection, data poisoning, or poor permission design.
examples: OWASP Agentic Top 10
Claude Code Security
OpenAI deployment simulation tool-use rollouts

risks.name: Model/vendor access instability
risks.description: Frontier vendors can change access, safety gates, pricing, or regional availability abruptly, leaving dependent systems without continuity.
examples: Anthropic Fable 5 suspension and redeployment
Regional access constraints on frontier models

risks.name: Companion-bot harm and synthetic-media abuse
risks.description: Consumer-facing AI companions and AI-generated media can expose children and adults to sexual content, coercion, impersonation, fraud, and harassment.
examples: eSafety report on AI companions
eSafety deepfake guidance
eSafety notices to AI companion providers
Deepfake sextortion and fabricated sexual content cases

risks.name: ADM Transparency and Privacy Non-Compliance Risk
risks.description: Organisations that use computer programs and personal information to make decisions affecting rights or interests face a defined Australian transparency deadline. The practical risk is incomplete discovery, incomplete privacy-policy updates, and weak ownership across business, privacy, and engineering teams.
examples: Automated loan or eligibility decisions without clear disclosure
Hiring or pricing decisions made by computer programs

risks.name: AI-Accelerated Cybercrime and Fraud Risk
risks.description: Frontier AI lowers the barrier to sophisticated phishing, scam generation, vulnerability discovery, and social engineering. Regulators are now explicitly telling supervised entities to treat AI as a cyber multiplier rather than a separate issue.
examples: AI-generated investment scams and cloaked social ads
AI-assisted credential theft and social engineering

risks.name: Companion-Bot Child Safety and Harm Risk
risks.description: AI companion products can expose minors to sexually explicit content, coercive interactions, and unsafe emotional dependence. eSafety’s notices indicate this is already a live consumer protection issue in Australia.
examples: Companion bot producing sexually explicit content to a minor
Chatbot encouraging harmful or dependency-forming interactions

risks.name: Benchmark and Evaluation Overconfidence Risk
risks.description: Traditional benchmark evaluations can overstate readiness if they are not statistically valid or deployment-like. OpenAI’s deployment simulation and NIST’s evaluation draft both show the market is still working out how to measure real-world behavior reliably.
examples: Benchmarks that do not reflect production behavior
Release decisions based on non-reproducible evals

risks.name: Agent Privilege Escalation
risks.description: Tool-using AI systems can overreach their permissions, chain actions unexpectedly, or be manipulated through prompt injection and malicious tool inputs.
examples: Prompt injection leading to unintended tool calls
Agent-initiated changes to production systems without explicit approval

risks.name: ADM Transparency Non-Compliance
risks.description: Organisations using ADM for high-impact decisions may miss the 10 December 2026 privacy-policy disclosure requirement or fail to identify which workflows are in scope.
examples: Automated credit, hiring, insurance, or eligibility decisions without clear disclosures
Privacy policies that omit ADM-specific detail

risks.name: AI-Accelerated Cybercrime and Fraud
risks.description: Frontier AI lowers the cost and increases the scale of phishing, social engineering, vulnerability discovery, and fraud campaigns.
examples: ASIC warning about frontier-AI-enabled cyber risk
AI-generated fraudulent content and impersonation

risks.name: Regulatory Fragmentation and Conditional Deferral
risks.description: Cross-border AI compliance remains fragmented, and some EU AI Act changes are still provisional until formal adoption, creating timeline uncertainty for multinational organisations.
examples: EU Digital Omnibus on AI postponing parts of the timetable
Different obligations for Australia, EU, and US deployers

controls.name: AI Use Case Inventory and Risk Classification
controls.description: Comprehensive cataloguing of all AI use cases across the organisation with classification by risk level (minimal, limited, high-risk, unacceptable per EU AI Act or equivalent).
controls.implementation: Build and maintain a register of all AI systems, their purpose, data inputs, decisions made, and affected stakeholders. Classify by risk tier. Review quarterly.
controls.owner: Chief Risk Officer / Head of AI Governance
controls.evidenceRequired: AI inventory register with risk classifications, review dates, and change history
controls.testingMethod: Quarterly audit of AI inventory completeness and classification accuracy
controls.limitations: Requires organisational commitment and resources. May miss shadow AI deployments.

controls.name: AI Acceptable-Use Policy
controls.risk: Shadow AI and Ungoverned AI Adoption
controls.description: Organisational policy defining permitted and prohibited AI uses, data handling requirements, approval workflows, and consequences for non-compliance.
controls.implementation: Develop and communicate policy covering: approved AI tools, prohibited uses (e.g., sensitive data input), employee responsibilities, reporting obligations, and enforcement mechanisms.
controls.evidenceRequired: Published policy, employee acknowledgement records, training completion records
controls.testingMethod: Periodic employee awareness testing and monitoring for unauthorised AI tool usage
controls.limitations: Cannot prevent all shadow AI use. Requires technical controls (DLP, network monitoring) for enforcement.

controls.name: MCP SDK Version Audit and Patching
controls.description: Inventory all MCP servers and SDK versions across the organisation. Apply available patches from Anthropic. Monitor for new advisories. The architectural nature of the flaw means version patching is a necessary but potentially insufficient control.
controls.implementation: Automated scanning for MCP SDK versions; dependency management with version pinning; patch management SLA
controls.testingMethod: Automated dependency scanning; penetration testing of MCP server deployments
controls.limitations: Architectural fix may require MCP protocol redesign; version patching is partial mitigation

controls.name: MCP Server Network Segmentation
controls.description: Deploy MCP servers in isolated network segments with strict egress controls. Prevent lateral movement from compromised MCP endpoints. Implement zero-trust networking for agent-to-server communication.
controls.implementation: Network microsegmentation; egress filtering; mTLS for agent-server authentication
controls.testingMethod: Network segmentation testing; penetration testing from compromised MCP server
controls.limitations: Segmentation is a compensating control — does not fix the underlying vulnerability

controls.name: AI Impact Assessment (DTA Standard)
controls.description: Standardised AI impact assessment covering fairness, safety, privacy, transparency, security, and human-centred values before AI deployment. Required by Australian DTA policy for government AI use cases.
controls.implementation: Use DTA's published AI impact assessment tool template. Complete assessment before deployment, review annually, and update on material change
controls.evidenceRequired: Completed AI impact assessment document; accountable owner sign-off
controls.testingMethod: Internal audit review; DTA compliance check
controls.frequency: Before deployment; annual review; event-driven updates
controls.limitations: Assessment quality depends on assessor expertise; does not address runtime risks

controls.name: Agent tool-call security gateway
controls.risk: Agent tool-parameter injection leading to code execution
controls.description: Central enforcement layer for all agent tool calls that validates schemas, blocks unsafe parameters, applies policy and records evidence before execution.
controls.implementation: Place a policy gateway between the model planner and tools; enforce allowlists, type/range validation, path restrictions, command denial, secrets masking and approval workflows.
controls.evidenceRequired: Tool inventory, gateway policy config, blocked-call logs, test results for prompt-injection payloads, approval records
controls.testingMethod: Red-team direct and indirect prompt injection; unit-test schema validators; replay malicious documents/emails/web content
controls.frequency: Continuous logging with quarterly red-team review
relatedStandards: NIST AI RMF Generative AI Profile
controls.limitations: Does not eliminate model deception or business-logic misuse; requires comprehensive tool inventory and continuous update.

controls.name: AI privacy deployment assessment
controls.description: Privacy assessment specifically for commercial AI tools before approval and during material changes.
controls.implementation: Assess necessity, personal-information flows, vendor retention/training, transparency notices, accuracy/explainability, public chatbot restrictions and human review for adverse decisions.
controls.evidenceRequired: Completed assessment, vendor terms, data-flow map, user notice, control attestations, residual risk approval
controls.testingMethod: Sample prompts and outputs for personal information leakage; review vendor configuration; verify deletion/retention settings
controls.frequency: Before deployment and annually or upon material vendor/model change
controls.limitations: Depends on vendor transparency and may not catch downstream inference or emergent model behaviour.

controls.name: Agentic AI Human Intervention and Safe Shutdown
controls.description: Design and test mechanisms that let authorised humans monitor, pause, alter or stop agentic AI activity and recover safely.
controls.implementation: For each agentic system, define authorised supervisors, escalation thresholds, real-time monitoring signals, per-tool permissions, memory/data boundaries, stop/pause commands, rollback procedures and incident logging. Test the control before production and after material system changes.
controls.owner: Product owner with Security, Risk and Operations
controls.evidenceRequired: Architecture diagram, tool-permission matrix, supervisor roster, stop/pause test logs, monitoring dashboard, incident playbook and audit trail.
controls.testingMethod: Scenario-based red-team or tabletop: trigger unexpected tool use, memory misuse, goal drift and resource consumption; verify humans can detect, intervene and stop the system.
controls.frequency: Before deployment, quarterly for high-risk agents, and after material changes
controls.maturity: Emerging but required for high-autonomy deployments
relatedStandards: DTA Agentic AI addendum to the AI technical standard
controls.limitations: Human intervention controls can fail if telemetry is incomplete, authority is unclear, or the agent acts faster than monitoring/approval processes.

controls.name: ADM Transparency Readiness Review
controls.description: Pre-commencement review of systems that use personal information in automated or substantially automated decisions that significantly affect rights or interests.
controls.implementation: Inventory ADM systems; classify personal information, decision type, significance and automation role; update APP privacy policy disclosures; map redress and complaint pathways; retain legal sign-off.
controls.evidenceRequired: ADM register, classification rationale, privacy policy draft, stakeholder review, approval record and change log.
controls.testingMethod: Sample high-impact decisions and verify disclosures accurately describe personal information and decision kinds.
controls.frequency: Initial review by September 2026 guidance; then annually and when systems change
relatedStandards: OAIC ADM Transparency Obligation Issues Paper
controls.limitations: Final OAIC guidance is expected later in 2026; interpretations may change.

controls.name: OWASP Agentic Top 10 Compliance Programme
controls.risk: AI Agent Supply Chain Exploitation (MCP, Skills Marketplaces)
controls.description: Adopt the OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications 2026 (December 2025) as a control baseline for AI agent security. Cover the ten risk categories across agent design, deployment, and operations. Track the upcoming OWASP Agentic Skills Top 10 (v1.0 Q3 2026) for skill-content security.
controls.implementation: Map existing AI agent deployments to each of the ten OWASP Agentic categories. For each category, identify responsible team, current state, gap, remediation, evidence, and review date. Include in the agent security assessment workflow and procurement checklist.
controls.owner: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Head of AI Security
controls.evidenceRequired: Completed Agentic Top 10 control assessment per agent deployment, with evidence per control and remediation tracking
controls.testingMethod: Annual third-party assessment + continuous internal control testing; red-team exercise covering prompt injection, agent identity, supply chain, and exfiltration paths
relatedTools: MCP server security scanners (e.g., community tools)
controls.limitations: Agentic Top 10 is new (December 2025) and will evolve. Skills Top 10 is still in proposal. Reference implementations and certified tools are limited.

controls.name: EU AI Act Digital Omnibus Compliance Re-baseline
controls.risk: EU AI Act Deferral Misinterpretation Risk
controls.description: Re-baseline the EU AI Act compliance programme against the Digital Omnibus on AI provisional deal (7 May 2026) without pausing existing readiness work. Treat deferral as conditional and prepare for adoption before 2 August 2026.
controls.implementation: Update internal AI Act compliance timeline: Annex III HRAIS 2 December 2027, Annex I HRAIS 2 August 2028, Article 50(2) transparency 2 December 2026, national sandboxes 2 August 2027. Continue readiness for in-force obligations: prohibited practices, GPAI rules, transparency, fundamental rights impact assessments. Track formal adoption progress (Council, Parliament).
controls.owner: Chief Compliance Officer / Head of AI Governance
controls.evidenceRequired: Updated AI Act compliance timeline; gap assessment against new dates; monitoring reports on Digital Omnibus adoption progress; legal review of provisional vs enacted status
controls.testingMethod: Quarterly compliance progress reviews; legal review of Digital Omnibus adoption status
controls.frequency: Quarterly review, immediate update on any legislative change
relatedStandards: EU AI Act Code of Practice for GPAI
controls.limitations: Provisional status means deferral could be modified or reversed. Sector-specific product regulations in Annex I may have separate compliance deadlines.

controls.name: APP 1 ADM Transparency Disclosure (OAIC 10 December 2026)
controls.risk: Privacy non-compliance and erosion of public trust in ADM
controls.description: Mandatory disclosure in APP entities' privacy policies of the types of personal information used in, and types of decisions made by, computer programs that use personal information to make decisions that could significantly affect an individual's rights or interests. The OAIC's 18 May 2026 Issues Paper seeks feedback on edge cases including generative AI with human oversight, differential pricing, and third-party ADM.
controls.implementation: Update privacy policy template; identify in-scope ADM use cases; align disclosures with OAIC Issues Paper edge cases; add new disclosures to privacy policy before 10 December 2026; document decision on third-party ADM and 'arranged for' status.
controls.evidenceRequired: Updated privacy policy with ADM section, ADM inventory mapping to APP entities, sign-off record, complaints handling log, OAIC Issues Paper feedback log.
controls.testingMethod: Mock complaint scenario; OAIC submission walkthrough; sample ADM user notice review; bias and accuracy testing on the disclosed decisions.
controls.frequency: Annual review, ad-hoc on use case change
controls.limitations: Privacy policy is one channel; users may not see disclosures; the OAIC's final guidance on generative AI edge cases is pending; cross-jurisdictional ADM disclosures (EU, US) may differ.

controls.name: AI use-case inventory and risk tiering
controls.risk: AI governance and supplier risk in regulated entities
controls.description: Maintain a living register of AI systems, owners, vendors, data types, and risk tiers.
controls.implementation: Tie procurement, change management, and incident reporting into a single register reviewed by risk and board committees.
controls.testingMethod: Sample completeness testing and owner attestation
controls.limitations: Inventory quality depends on procurement and change discipline.

controls.name: ADM transparency workflow
controls.description: Ensure privacy policies and decision explanations capture ADM use that may affect rights or interests.
controls.implementation: Add a pre-release privacy review step to every ADM use case and version-controlled disclosure templates.
controls.testingMethod: Spot-check live systems against published disclosures
controls.limitations: Hard to maintain if product teams bypass governance gates.

controls.name: Agentic AI red-teaming and tool-abuse testing
controls.risk: Agentic workflow failure and tool abuse
controls.description: Test autonomy boundaries, permission scopes, and tool-call behavior before production release.
controls.implementation: Run attack scenarios covering prompt injection, data exfiltration, and unsafe action execution.
controls.testingMethod: Pre-release red-team and periodic re-test
controls.limitations: Coverage can miss emergent multi-step failure modes.

controls.name: Out-of-band verification for high-risk transactions
controls.description: Require a second channel of verification for money movement, identity changes, and sensitive approvals.
controls.implementation: Pair voice/video or message-based requests with independent callbacks or signed approvals.
controls.testingMethod: Fraud scenario drills and sample audits
controls.limitations: Adds friction and may not stop fast-moving social engineering alone.

controls.name: Vendor assurance pack for frontier models
controls.risk: AI-accelerated cyber threats and misuse of frontier models
controls.description: Require vendors to disclose testing, safety controls, incident handling, and security hardening.
controls.implementation: Add assurance requirements to procurement and renewals, with contractual audit and notification rights.
controls.testingMethod: Document review and control testing
controls.limitations: Vendor disclosures can be incomplete without independent validation.

controls.name: Agentic vendor assurance and access governance
controls.description: Control the use of frontier-model partners and embedded engineers in regulated workflows by requiring evidence of access controls, logging, escalation paths, and audit rights before production rollout.
controls.implementation: Run a formal partner review, map privileged access, separate pilot and production environments, require incident notification clauses, and test rollback and human-override paths before go-live.
controls.testingMethod: Contract review, access sampling, tabletop exercises, and scenario-based red-teaming
controls.limitations: Vendor telemetry and partner certifications do not prove safe real-world operation.

controls.name: AI trust and liability disclosure workflow
controls.description: Create a disclosure and evidence workflow for rights-affecting AI decisions so that legal, privacy, and communications teams can explain what the system does and who is accountable.
controls.implementation: Maintain an ADM and AI-use inventory, map each use case to disclosures, assign accountable owners, and rehearse complaint handling and escalation.
controls.testingMethod: Policy review, sample disclosure testing, and incident/complaint tabletop exercises
controls.limitations: Disclosure alone does not reduce model risk or liability exposure.

controls.name: AI system inventory and risk tiering
controls.description: Maintain a live register of AI use cases, owners, data dependencies, decision impact, and risk tier.
controls.implementation: Create one inventory for all AI-assisted and automated decision workflows; require business owner, technical owner, model/vendor, and data classification fields.
controls.evidenceRequired: Current inventory, review history, risk tier rationale, and approvals
controls.testingMethod: Quarterly sample check against deployed systems and procurement records
controls.limitations: Can drift out of date unless procurement and engineering are tightly linked.

controls.name: AI pre-launch assurance gate
controls.description: Require explicit assurance sign-off before any high-impact or externally facing AI feature goes live.
controls.implementation: Gate launch on documented testing, fallback/rollback, review of bias and safety issues, and sign-off from legal, security, and business owners.
controls.evidenceRequired: Test results, sign-off record, rollback plan, and issue log
controls.testingMethod: Release audit and spot-check on sampled launches
controls.limitations: Slows releases if teams do not standardise test packs and approval workflows.

controls.name: AI-specific cyber red-team coverage
controls.description: Test prompt injection, tool misuse, data leakage, and agent privilege escalation before deployment.
controls.implementation: Add AI attack scenarios to red-team exercises and require remediation for any path that can alter tools, data, or identity state.
controls.evidenceRequired: Red-team reports, remediations, and retest evidence
controls.testingMethod: Tabletop plus adversarial testing
controls.frequency: At launch and after major changes
controls.limitations: Needs recurring re-testing as tool access and model behaviour change.

controls.name: ADM transparency and appeal workflow
controls.description: Prepare disclosures, human review paths, and appeal processes for automated decisions that affect rights or entitlements.
controls.implementation: Inventory ADM use, identify disclosure triggers, draft customer notices, and create escalation/appeal routes before the December 2026 obligation date.
controls.evidenceRequired: ADM inventory, notice templates, and approval records
controls.testingMethod: Pre-implementation review and customer journey walkthrough
controls.frequency: Every new use case; annual refresh
controls.limitations: Disclosure is only useful if users can actually understand and contest the decision.

controls.name: ADM Disclosure and Contestability Workflow
controls.description: Map which decisions are made by computer programs and whether they could significantly affect rights or interests, then disclose the relevant categories in privacy policies.
controls.implementation: Create a decision-impact taxonomy, align legal/privacy review, and update policy text and customer notices before the deadline.
controls.testingMethod: Sample-audit decisions to confirm that disclosures match actual workflows
controls.limitations: Hard to maintain if decision workflows change faster than policy updates.

controls.name: Continuous AI Security Monitoring and Prompt Regression Testing
controls.description: Monitor deployed AI for drift, prompt injection, tool abuse, and anomalous agent actions after release.
controls.implementation: Instrument model and tool calls, alert on unusual patterns, log prompts and outputs, and run periodic regression and red-team tests.
controls.evidenceRequired: Alerts, logs, red-team reports, and post-incident actions
controls.testingMethod: Simulated attacks, canary prompts, production telemetry review
controls.limitations: Can miss novel attacks and requires good telemetry.

controls.name: Synthetic-Media Provenance and Impersonation Response
controls.description: A fraud-and-integrity control that combines provenance checks, platform escalation, and rapid customer communication when deepfake content appears.
controls.implementation: Track impersonation attempts, verify source provenance, pre-authorise takedown contacts, and maintain public-warning templates for brand or public-figure abuse.
controls.evidenceRequired: Takedown logs, provenance checks, incident timelines, stakeholder notifications, legal escalation records
controls.testingMethod: Deepfake incident tabletop; takedown drill; social monitoring replay; brand-impersonation scenario test
controls.limitations: Detection can lag generation; provenance depends on ecosystem adoption and may not be available for every asset.

controls.name: Sandboxed agent containment with egress control
controls.description: Constrain agent actions inside ephemeral containers, sandboxes, or local VMs and restrict external network reach so one failure cannot cascade across the enterprise.
controls.implementation: Use isolated runtimes per task, pre-approved tool allow-lists, explicit outbound network policies, and audit logging for every privileged action.
controls.evidenceRequired: Architecture diagram, sandbox policy, outbound firewall policy, and test logs showing blocked abuse attempts.
controls.testingMethod: Red-team tool-use abuse, permission-failure drills, and recovery testing after forced termination.
controls.frequency: Each release plus quarterly control review
controls.limitations: Containment reduces blast radius but does not eliminate model error or insider misuse.

controls.name: Continuous-monitor-and-update AI security program
controls.description: Treat AI security as an operating programme rather than a one-time review, with recurring prompt testing, adversary emulation, and patching of guardrails.
controls.implementation: Maintain a standing queue of regression prompts, monitor for new bypasses, and feed findings into release gates and incident response.
controls.evidenceRequired: Test logs, regression backlog, patch records, and incident tickets showing update cycles.
controls.testingMethod: Monthly adversary emulation and continuous prompt regression.
controls.limitations: Requires steady resourcing and does not fully solve novel attack techniques.

controls.name: Board-level AI inventory and accountability map
controls.risk: Governance Implementation Lag in Regulated Australian Entities
controls.description: Create a board-approved inventory of AI use cases, owners, decision rights, data dependencies, and risk tiers so governance can be tested against prudential expectations.
controls.implementation: Integrate AI inventory into enterprise GRC, assign accountable owners, and tie high-risk uses to committee review and documented sign-off.
controls.evidenceRequired: Current inventory, risk-tiering model, committee minutes, and sign-off evidence for high-risk use cases.
controls.testingMethod: Quarterly inventory reconciliation and sample testing of approvals and overrides.
controls.limitations: Inventory quality depends on business self-reporting and procurement coverage.

controls.name: SBOM and dependency integrity verification
controls.risk: Software Supply-Chain Integrity Gaps in AI Stacks
controls.description: Track dependencies and verify component integrity so AI applications and supporting services can be recovered and audited after a supply-chain issue.
controls.implementation: Ingest SBOMs at build time, scan transitive dependencies, and gate release on high-severity findings or unverified components.
controls.evidenceRequired: SBOMs, dependency scan reports, exception approvals, and release gates.
controls.testingMethod: Build-pipeline checks and periodic dependency drift review.
