controls.limitations: Only as strong as the completeness and freshness of the SBOM inputs.

controls.name: Live AI inventory and use-case register
controls.description: Maintain a continuously updated inventory of AI systems, owners, data sources, decision impact, and approval status.
controls.implementation: Tie the register to procurement, architecture, and change-management workflows so new use cases cannot go live without ownership and risk classification.
controls.evidenceRequired: Current register, named owners, approval records, and review cadence
controls.testingMethod: Sample audit of new AI deployments against the register
controls.limitations: Only works if business units disclose shadow AI and updates are enforced

controls.name: Board AI literacy and challenge pack
controls.description: Provide boards with a concise pack covering use cases, owners, risk appetite, controls, incidents, and vendor concentration.
controls.implementation: Use a standing board agenda item and require management to report on changes in AI risk posture and unresolved gaps.
controls.evidenceRequired: Board papers, challenge notes, follow-up actions, and decision logs
controls.testingMethod: Quarterly board-effectiveness review
controls.limitations: Can degrade into a reporting exercise unless directors ask hard questions

controls.name: Agent containment and least privilege
controls.risk: Agentic AI compromise and data leakage
controls.description: Constrain agents with sandboxing, explicit tool permissions, egress controls, and secrets isolation.
controls.implementation: Separate agent execution from core systems, log every tool call, and restrict what the agent can read, write, and exfiltrate.
controls.evidenceRequired: Architecture diagrams, access-control lists, sandbox logs, and egress policy evidence
controls.testingMethod: Red-team prompt injection and privilege escalation drills
controls.frequency: Per release and after major model/tool changes
controls.limitations: Hard to implement across heterogeneous tool stacks without architecture changes

controls.name: Prompt-injection and supply-chain red teaming
controls.risk: Agentic AI compromise and data leakage
controls.description: Continuously test prompts, retrieval paths, plugins, and third-party integrations for injection and dependency abuse.
controls.implementation: Add adversarial test cases to CI/CD and maintain a regression suite for known exploit patterns.
controls.evidenceRequired: Red-team reports, test coverage, fix tracking, and regression results
controls.testingMethod: Controlled exploit simulations and recurring adversarial testing
controls.limitations: Coverage will always lag novel attacker techniques unless maintained continuously

controls.name: Transparency, human review, and contestability controls
controls.risk: Transparency and contestability gap in AI decisioning
controls.description: Require notice when AI is used, disclosure when personal information is shared with third-party AI providers, human review, bias testing, and a path to challenge AI-mediated decisions.
controls.implementation: Add to product flows, privacy notices, complaints handling, and case management systems; tie each use case to an accountable official or executive sponsor.
controls.evidenceRequired: Published notice text, workflow screenshots, test cases, and audit logs.
controls.testingMethod: Scenario testing with sample decisions and appeal handling
controls.limitations: Human review can be superficial unless reviewers have authority and evidence.

controls.name: Agent containment baseline
controls.description: Constrain what an AI agent can do with tools, data, and credentials, and require a tested shutdown path.
controls.implementation: Use least-privilege tool scopes, sandbox execution, network egress filtering, explicit approval gates for destructive actions, and kill-switch drills.
controls.evidenceRequired: Tool-permission inventory, red-team results, shutdown test logs, incident playbook
controls.testingMethod: Adversarial prompt-injection tests, privilege-escalation tests, rollback drills
controls.limitations: Will not eliminate all misuse; requires continuous tuning as agent capability changes.

controls.name: High-impact AI review gate
controls.description: Require human review or explicit escalation paths where AI advice or decisions can materially affect a person’s safety, rights, or finances.
controls.implementation: Add severity thresholds, mandatory human handoff, complaint routes, and reviewable decision logs for high-impact workflows.
controls.evidenceRequired: Decision logs, human-review metrics, complaint handling evidence, safety incident reports
controls.testingMethod: Scenario testing with vulnerable-user prompts and escalation drills
controls.limitations: Human review can be slow; design must avoid creating a false promise of perfect safety.

controls.name: Provenance and disclosure controls
controls.risk: Synthetic Media and AI Slop Proliferation
controls.description: Mark synthetic content, preserve provenance metadata where possible, and clearly disclose AI assistance in publishing workflows.
controls.implementation: Use content labels, watermarking where supported, editorial review, and publication rules for AI-assisted output.
controls.evidenceRequired: Label coverage, provenance logs, editorial QA samples
controls.testingMethod: Random audits of published content and takedown/refusal rates
controls.limitations: Provenance can be stripped or lost across platforms; requires ecosystem support.

controls.name: AI incident logging and rollback
controls.description: Ensure every material AI workflow can be traced, paused, and rolled back quickly after an incident or policy breach.
controls.implementation: Log prompts, tool calls, model versions, policy decisions, and operator overrides in a searchable system of record.
controls.evidenceRequired: Complete logs, access records, rollback test results, incident postmortems
controls.testingMethod: Tabletop exercises and sample incident replays
controls.limitations: Logging alone is not containment; must be paired with response authority and controls.

controls.name: Age-assurance and account-gating workflow
controls.risk: Age-assurance and Social Media Minimum Age compliance failure
controls.description: Create a classification and enforcement workflow for in-scope social services, with documented reasonable steps, age checks, appeals, and monitoring.
controls.implementation: Map products against the eSafety definition, design age checks, log decisions, and rehearse exception handling before enforcement deadlines.
controls.evidenceRequired: Classification decisions, policy approvals, test cases, logs, appeals data, and incident records.
controls.testingMethod: Tabletop exercises, end-to-end user journey tests, and periodic audit of enforcement logs.
relatedStandards: eSafety Social Media Minimum Age requirements
controls.limitations: Age checks can create friction, false positives, and privacy trade-offs; effectiveness depends on the platform’s product design and user base.

controls.name: Synthetic-image abuse intake and takedown workflow
controls.risk: Synthetic image abuse and intimate-image extortion
controls.description: Set up a dedicated channel to receive, triage, preserve evidence, and remove intimate-image abuse or synthetic-media harassment rapidly.
controls.implementation: Create intake forms, escalation playbooks, legal review steps, and liaison paths to platforms, search engines, and victim-support services.
controls.evidenceRequired: Case logs, takedown SLAs, evidence-preservation steps, and victim-support referrals.
controls.testingMethod: Red-team abuse scenarios, moderation drills, and sample-case audits.
controls.frequency: Monthly review and after every major incident
controls.limitations: Removal can be slow across mirrored copies; synthetic content detection is imperfect and must be paired with response workflows.

controls.name: AI transparency statement and accountable-official workflow
controls.description: Keep an agency-wide register of AI use cases, assign accountable officials, and refresh transparency statements on a fixed cadence.
controls.implementation: Assign an accountable owner, catalogue use cases, link each to an executive sponsor, and publish transparent summaries of AI use and controls.
controls.owner: Chief Operating Officer / AI Branch / Policy
controls.evidenceRequired: Register extracts, transparency statements, board reporting, and review records.
controls.testingMethod: Quarterly control review and internal audit sampling.
relatedStandards: Australian Government policy for the responsible use of AI in government
controls.limitations: A transparency statement is only effective if it is current, complete, and tied to real operational ownership.

controls.name: Named accountable owner and AI transparency statement
controls.description: Assign a named owner for AI oversight and publish a short statement describing AI use, purpose, and accountability.
controls.implementation: Maintain a live AI inventory, define approval thresholds, and review the statement at board/risk cadence.
controls.evidenceRequired: Owner name, inventory entries, approval records, and current transparency statement.
controls.testingMethod: Internal audit and sampling of a few live AI use cases.
controls.limitations: Disclosure can be accurate yet still miss shadow AI or downstream misuse.

controls.name: Prompt-injection-resistant agent design
controls.risk: Agentic prompt injection and tool abuse
controls.description: Design agent workflows so untrusted input cannot directly drive destructive or sensitive actions.
controls.implementation: Use tool allowlists, separate read/write credentials, content sanitisation, and approval gates for high-impact actions.
controls.evidenceRequired: Red-team results, signed-off threat model, and logs showing gated sensitive actions.
controls.testingMethod: Scenario-based red-teaming with adversarial prompts and poisoned retrieval content.
controls.frequency: Per release and after major prompt/tool changes
relatedStandards: OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications
controls.limitations: No control fully prevents model error or user misuse; least privilege remains essential.

controls.name: AI-abuse telemetry and MITRE mapping
controls.description: Track observed AI misuse patterns and map them to adversary tactics so defenders can prioritise controls.
controls.implementation: Log abuse cases, classify by tactic, and feed results into SOC detection and policy tuning.
controls.evidenceRequired: Abuse taxonomy, detections, incident records, and periodic threat reports.
controls.testingMethod: Tabletop exercises and detection-engine drills tied to current threat patterns.
controls.limitations: Detection is retrospective and will miss novel abuse until patterns are learned.

controls.name: Scoped channel and tool access for collaborative agents
controls.description: Limit each agent to selected channels, data sources, and tools, and keep read-only access separate from destructive actions.
controls.implementation: Use allow-lists, IAM roles, and workspace/channel scoping; log all tool calls and surfaced context.
controls.evidenceRequired: Access matrix, permission review, audit log samples, and red-team test results.
controls.testingMethod: Prompt-injection and privilege-escalation tests across real workflows.
controls.frequency: Every release and after material permission changes
controls.limitations: Still depends on humans avoiding over-broad channel and tool grants.

controls.name: Human approval gate for destructive actions
controls.description: Require explicit human approval before an agent can delete, spend, publish, or otherwise take high-impact actions.
controls.implementation: Separate read and write paths; require approvals for write/delete/spend workflows; block autonomous escalation by default.
controls.evidenceRequired: Approval logs, blocked-action records, and change tickets.
controls.testingMethod: Adversarial task simulation and rollback drills.
controls.limitations: Can slow legitimate work if not carefully tuned.

controls.name: AI inventory and transparency statement control
controls.risk: Privacy and trust gap in data-driven AI use
controls.description: Maintain a live inventory of AI use cases and a disclosure template that names accountable owners, purpose, oversight, and escalation paths.
controls.implementation: Refresh the register quarterly; publish or internalise a transparency statement; align ownership with board reporting.
controls.evidenceRequired: Register entries, named owners, disclosure record, and board reporting pack.
controls.testingMethod: Disclosure spot checks and audit sampling.
controls.limitations: Disclosure alone does not guarantee technical safety or privacy compliance.

controls.name: Privacy minimisation and deletion workflow
controls.risk: Privacy and trust gap in data-driven AI use
controls.description: Reduce personal data exposure by default, restrict retention, and ensure deletion requests can be executed and evidenced.
controls.implementation: Apply data-classification rules to prompts, retrieval stores, logs, and training corpora; enforce retention windows.
controls.evidenceRequired: Retention settings, deletion logs, DPIAs, and exception approvals.
controls.testingMethod: Sampling of deletion requests and retention-policy audits.
controls.limitations: Hard to guarantee across downstream vendors and cached artifacts.

controls.name: Cyber-abuse telemetry and response playbook
controls.description: Monitor for malicious use patterns, map abuse to technique taxonomies, and define escalation paths for abuse, fraud, and account compromise.
controls.implementation: Track suspicious tool use, model prompts, and account behaviour; integrate with SOC triage and abuse review.
controls.evidenceRequired: Abuse dashboards, incident tickets, blocked-account reports, and remediation records.
controls.testingMethod: Purple-team exercises and simulated abuse cases.
controls.limitations: Detection coverage may lag adversarial adaptation.

controls.name: AI chatbot safety gate
controls.description: Require age-appropriate controls, crisis routing, and clear disclosures for any chatbot or companion feature that can influence vulnerable users.
controls.implementation: Insert safety prompts, block harmful content classes, route self-harm or abuse signals to humans, and log risky interactions for review.
controls.testingMethod: Scenario tests with vulnerable-user prompts and crisis-intent cases
controls.limitations: Cannot eliminate harm if the product is designed for open-ended companionship.

controls.name: Agent tool-use containment
controls.description: Constrain tool-using assistants to approved actions, scoped permissions, and human approval for high-impact operations.
controls.implementation: Use allowlists, sandboxed execution, secret isolation, and step-up approval for external side effects.
controls.testingMethod: Prompt-injection and abuse-path testing
controls.limitations: Hard to prove complete safety when tools can reach live systems.

controls.name: Provenance and human-verification workflow
controls.risk: Synthetic content, AI slop, and misinformation
controls.description: Require provenance checks and human review before publishing or acting on AI-generated text, images, audio, or video.
controls.implementation: Attach source metadata, verify high-impact claims, and block or label suspect synthetic media.
controls.testingMethod: Spot checks and claim verification exercises
controls.limitations: Provenance systems are only useful if upstream content carries usable signals.

controls.name: AI obligations map and change watch
controls.description: Maintain a living map of AI-related obligations across Australia, the EU, and sectoral regulators.
controls.implementation: Link each AI use case to controls, evidence, owners, and review dates; update on every material policy change.
controls.testingMethod: Internal audit and spot-checks against current guidance
controls.limitations: Requires continual monitoring; stale mappings become misleading quickly.

controls.name: Vendor due-diligence evidence pack
controls.risk: Vendor concentration and safety posture drift
controls.description: Ask vendors for evidence of safety testing, transparency, logging, incident response, and change-notification practices.
controls.implementation: Collect product docs, evaluation results, security attestations, and contractual commitments before purchase.
controls.testingMethod: Procurement review and quarterly vendor reassessment
controls.limitations: Vendor documentation can lag product reality; independent testing is still needed.

controls.name: AI review gate
controls.risk: Compliance lag across fast-moving AI guidance
controls.description: Require structured review and sign-off before any new AI use case is approved or scaled.
controls.implementation: Create a checklist for purpose, data, model, human oversight, logs, and risk acceptance.
controls.testingMethod: Sample approvals and verify evidence completeness
controls.limitations: Only works if teams actually route AI projects through it.

controls.name: Agent containment and approvals
controls.risk: Agentic AI misuse and unsafe automation
controls.description: Keep autonomous systems inside clear permission bounds and require human approval for material actions.
controls.implementation: Use allowlists, sandboxing, scoped credentials, and step-up approvals for external side effects.
controls.testingMethod: Prompt-injection and abuse-path testing
controls.limitations: Hard to guarantee safety if tools can act on live systems.

controls.name: Transparency-statement maintenance
controls.description: Tie each public AI statement to a current system inventory, owner, and review date.
controls.implementation: Update the statement on each major model, policy, or workflow change and keep a changelog.
controls.testingMethod: Quarterly review against actual AI deployments
controls.limitations: Statements can lag if change management is weak.

controls.name: Chatbot safety gate
controls.description: Add age-appropriate controls, crisis routing, and harm-minimisation checks to any consumer-facing chatbot or companion feature.
controls.implementation: Implement policy-based blocks, escalation paths, and warning/disclosure copy before launch.
controls.testingMethod: Scenario tests with vulnerable-user prompts
controls.limitations: Cannot fully eliminate harm in open-ended conversation products.

controls.name: Browser-agent least privilege and egress control
controls.description: Limit what a browser agent can see, click, download, and send so a compromised page cannot drive the system outside its intended scope.
controls.implementation: Use separate credentials, network egress allowlists, and a sandbox or VM for any browser-capable agent.
controls.evidenceRequired: Documented tool allowlist, sandbox design, and test logs showing blocked exfiltration paths.
controls.testingMethod: Prompt-injection and adversarial-page tests
controls.limitations: Does not remove the need for human approval on material actions.

controls.name: Human approval for material agent actions
controls.description: Require a person to approve external side effects such as sending messages, changing records, or making purchases.
controls.implementation: Implement approval queues and explicit confirmation UX for side-effecting steps.
controls.evidenceRequired: Approved workflow screenshots and audit logs showing who authorised each action.
controls.testingMethod: Workflow review and control walkthrough
controls.limitations: Can slow automation and may be bypassed if escalation paths are unclear.

controls.name: Model capability regression and exploit-eval gate
controls.description: Block releases or internal rollouts until the model passes a defined set of cyber, jailbreak, and exploit-development tests.
controls.implementation: Maintain a benchmark suite covering exploit chaining, prompt injection, and dangerous capability thresholds.
controls.evidenceRequired: Repeatable benchmark reports and threshold sign-off from model owners.
controls.testingMethod: Red-team benchmark runs and regression testing
controls.frequency: Every model or vendor version change
controls.limitations: Benchmarks lag reality and cannot fully predict novel abuse patterns.

controls.name: Vendor claims verification checklist
controls.risk: Vendor concentration and safety posture drift
controls.description: Make vendors prove security, safety, and operational claims before procurement or expansion.
controls.implementation: Request logs, eval results, incident processes, change notifications, and audit rights in contracts.
controls.evidenceRequired: Completed vendor due-diligence pack and contract clauses.
controls.testingMethod: Pre-procurement review and annual reassessment
controls.limitations: Vendor-provided evidence still needs independent validation where possible.

controls.name: Independent safety and standards review
controls.description: Keep internal obligations mapping aligned to external guidance so policy does not drift away from implementation.
controls.implementation: Run a quarterly review of AI inventory, obligations, and evidence against current guidance and standards.
controls.evidenceRequired: Reviewed obligations map and meeting minutes.
controls.testingMethod: Control testing and evidence review
controls.limitations: Useful only if the underlying AI inventory is current.

controls.name: Scoped Agent Access Control
controls.risk: Agentic Prompt Injection and Side Effects
controls.description: Give each agent the smallest feasible set of channels, tools, files, and permissions, and keep sensitive actions behind explicit approval gates.
controls.implementation: Use role- and channel-scoped access, tool allowlists, spend caps, and approval queues for side-effecting actions.
controls.evidenceRequired: Access matrices, logs, approval records, and exception register.
controls.testingMethod: Adversarial workflow tests and permission reviews.
controls.limitations: Does not prevent all prompt injection or user error.

controls.name: Agent Red-Teaming and Sandbox Review
controls.risk: LLM-Enabled Exploit Discovery and Dual-Use Cyber Acceleration
controls.description: Test agentic and cyber-capable systems in isolated environments before permitting them to touch real data, code, or infrastructure.
controls.implementation: Run red-team exercises, exploit-adjacent task containment, and human validation for all findings and patches.
controls.evidenceRequired: Red-team reports, benchmark results, and approved test logs.
controls.testingMethod: Controlled adversarial exercises and benchmark regression tests.
controls.limitations: Cannot guarantee safety against all future exploit techniques.

controls.name: Independent Assurance Gate
controls.description: Require evidence packs, external testing, and explicit sign-off before accepting vendor safety or standards claims.
controls.implementation: Mandate model cards, system cards, eval results, audit rights, incident reporting, and change notices.
controls.evidenceRequired: Signed assurance pack and third-party or internal validation results.
controls.testingMethod: Control-to-evidence review and procurement diligence.
controls.frequency: Before procurement and at material change
controls.limitations: Assurance quality still depends on the rigor of the tests used.

controls.name: Portability and Exit Readiness
controls.description: Reduce lock-in by keeping benchmarks, data, and workflows portable across vendors and infrastructure options.
controls.implementation: Benchmark multiple models, keep prompts and evals vendor-neutral where possible, and define exit plans in contracts.
controls.evidenceRequired: Benchmark comparisons, exit plans, and contract clauses.
controls.testingMethod: Periodic migration and fallback drills.
controls.limitations: Portability is harder when workflows depend on proprietary toolchains.

controls.name: Agent permission scoping and action logging
controls.description: Limit what agents can see and do, and keep a durable log of every material action they take.
controls.implementation: Use channel, repo, and tool-level permissions; require explicit approval for write or deploy actions; record prompt, tool use, and outcome.
controls.evidenceRequired: Permission matrix, approval workflow, immutable logs, and failed-abuse test results.
controls.testingMethod: Prompt-injection and unauthorized-action red-team tests.
controls.limitations: Logging alone does not stop side effects; human review is still needed for high-impact actions.

controls.name: Pre-release deployment simulation and eval gate
controls.risk: Assurance gap between vendor claims and buyer evidence
controls.description: Simulate production-like conditions before rollout so you can see how models behave on real tasks and failure modes.
controls.implementation: Run scenario replay, stress tests, and abuse-case evaluations before each major rollout or model upgrade.
controls.evidenceRequired: Evaluation pack, pass/fail thresholds, and documented failure analysis.
controls.testingMethod: Red-team harness, benchmark run, and deployment simulation.
controls.frequency: Every major release or policy change
controls.limitations: Vendor evaluation results may not generalise to your data, users, or operational constraints.

controls.name: Third-party assurance and procurement evidence gate
controls.risk: Assurance gap between vendor claims and buyer evidence
controls.description: Require vendors to supply evidence that can be independently checked before they become a standardised platform or critical supplier.
controls.implementation: Collect eval results, data-use terms, audit rights, incident-notification commitments, and exit clauses as procurement artefacts.
controls.evidenceRequired: Signed due-diligence pack, contractual controls, and verification record.
controls.testingMethod: Evidence review and contract-control testing.
controls.limitations: Procurement reviews can be too coarse if technical teams are not involved.

controls.name: Privacy impact and transparency checklist
controls.risk: Privacy trust erosion in customer and employee AI
controls.description: Make explainability, retention, consent, and notice quality explicit for any AI system that touches personal data.
controls.implementation: Run PIAs/DPIAs, update notices, minimise data collection, and define retention/delete workflows before launch.
controls.evidenceRequired: PIA/DPIA, notice text, retention schedule, and user support runbook.
controls.testingMethod: Sample AI journeys against the checklist and review complaints or opt-outs.
controls.frequency: Per use case and annual refresh
controls.limitations: Transparent notices do not substitute for minimisation and access controls.

controls.name: AI use-case register with named executive sponsor
controls.description: Maintain a live inventory of AI use cases, owners, sponsors, and risk ratings so no tool is deployed without accountability.
controls.implementation: Follow the DTA pattern: assign a system owner and an executive sponsor to every AI use case, and keep the register current.
controls.evidenceRequired: Current register, owner assignments, approval records, review dates
controls.testingMethod: Sample use cases and trace them from approval to production logs
controls.limitations: Only effective if teams actually keep the register updated

controls.name: Continuous monitoring and rollback for deployed AI
controls.description: Instrument AI systems so performance, safety, and security signals are visible after release and can trigger rollback.
controls.implementation: Attach telemetry, alerts, and update gates to every high-impact AI deployment; require incident playbooks.
controls.evidenceRequired: Monitoring dashboards, alert histories, rollback drills, incident logs
controls.testingMethod: Red-team and drift tests in staging plus live telemetry reviews
controls.limitations: Monitoring is only useful when owners are empowered to pause or roll back

controls.name: Human approval gates for high-impact outputs
controls.description: Require explicit human review before AI systems can send external communications, change code, or act on sensitive data.
controls.implementation: Use workflow approvals for code merges, external emails, procurement actions, and public content publication.
controls.evidenceRequired: Approval logs, exception records, policy settings
controls.testingMethod: Attempted bypass tests and sample control reviews
controls.limitations: May slow delivery if not scoped by risk tier

controls.name: Transparency statement and privacy notice refresh
controls.description: Publish plain-language explanations of AI data use, retention, and human oversight, especially where systems interact with the public.
controls.implementation: Align notices with the OAIC trust signal and the DTA transparency pattern; review every major AI release.
controls.evidenceRequired: Published notices, change log, stakeholder review record
controls.testingMethod: Readability review and privacy-impact assessment
controls.limitations: A good notice does not guarantee good practice

controls.name: AI / agent inventory with named owners
controls.risk: Shadow AI, concentration risk, and poor accountability
controls.description: Maintain a live inventory of every AI system, agent, model endpoint, and high-risk use case, with business owner, technical owner, vendor, and data classifications.
controls.implementation: Tie procurement, security, privacy, and change-management checkpoints to a required inventory record before any production launch.
controls.evidenceRequired: Current inventory export, owner assignments, and change history
controls.testingMethod: Spot-check production use cases against procurement and access logs
controls.limitations: Only works if teams declare shadow AI and refresh records when use cases change.

controls.name: Deployment simulation / replay testing
controls.risk: Static assurance gap for rapidly changing models
controls.description: Replay realistic conversations or workflows against candidate models before release to see how behavior changes in context and whether unsafe actions emerge.
controls.implementation: Use privacy-preserving replay, compare against baseline models, and block release if new high-risk behaviors appear.
controls.evidenceRequired: Simulation logs, comparison results, mitigation decisions
controls.testingMethod: Replay pre-release traffic and adversarial scenarios
controls.limitations: Cannot prove safety; it only improves the odds of catching failure modes before deployment.

controls.name: Least-privilege tool access and approval gating
controls.risk: Agentic AI privilege escalation and unintended action risk
controls.description: Restrict agents to the minimum tool permissions needed and require explicit approval for actions that move money, delete data, change safeguards, or contact external systems.
controls.implementation: Separate read, write, and execute scopes; add human sign-off for sensitive actions; and use short-lived credentials.
controls.evidenceRequired: Permission matrix, approval workflow, credential scope logs
controls.testingMethod: Adversarial task tests, permission audits, and rollback drills
controls.limitations: Can slow down workflows if approvals are too coarse or too slow.

controls.name: ADM transparency mapping and privacy-policy refresh
controls.description: Map every automated decision workflow that uses personal information and refresh privacy policies, notices, and complaint paths before the 10 December 2026 commencement date.
controls.implementation: Create a cross-functional workflow for legal, privacy, and product teams to classify decisions, data use, and human review.
controls.evidenceRequired: Workflow register, updated privacy policy, approval records
controls.testingMethod: Policy-to-system traceability audit
controls.frequency: Quarterly until compliance date, then annually
controls.limitations: Needs executive sponsorship because it touches multiple business functions.

controls.name: Supplier continuity and exit planning
controls.risk: Model concentration and vendor-access shock risk
controls.description: Design procurement and operational continuity so a vendor suspension, pricing shift, or regional change does not stop business-critical workflows.
controls.implementation: Maintain secondary models, portability of prompts and data, and tested fallback routes for critical processes.
controls.evidenceRequired: Exit plan, fallback test evidence, contract clauses
controls.testingMethod: Tabletop exercise and failover test
controls.limitations: Secondary providers may not match the primary model's quality or safety profile.

controls.name: Agent sandboxing and approval gates
controls.description: Keep agents in constrained environments with explicit tool allowlists and human approval for high-impact actions.
controls.implementation: Use least-privilege credentials, separate execution environments, and approval checkpoints before code writes, data exports, or external actions.
controls.evidenceRequired: Agent policy, permission map, approval logs, and test results from red-team scenarios
controls.testingMethod: Prompt-injection exercises, tool-abuse simulations, and rollback drills
controls.frequency: Every release and quarterly penetration test
relatedStandards: OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications for 2026
controls.limitations: Can slow workflows and still depends on good tool design

controls.name: ADM inventory and disclosure mapping
controls.description: Create a single inventory of AI-assisted and automated decisions and map them to disclosure obligations, human review, and contestability paths.
controls.implementation: Tag each workflow by data used, decision significance, rights impact, owner, and required privacy-policy wording.
controls.evidenceRequired: ADM register, policy mapping, privacy-policy update drafts, sign-off records
controls.testingMethod: Table-top review against sample decisions and legal review of borderline cases
controls.limitations: Requires cross-functional ownership and ongoing maintenance

controls.name: Model continuity and fallback routing
controls.description: Ensure critical workflows can fail over to another model or manual process when a vendor changes access, pricing, or safety gating.
controls.implementation: Maintain a fallback model list, routing rules, exit criteria, and a manual override for business-critical functions.
controls.evidenceRequired: Continuity plan, fallback test report, vendor exit terms, incident playbook
controls.testingMethod: Switch-over exercises and vendor outage simulations
controls.frequency: Twice yearly and after major vendor changes
controls.limitations: Multiple-model support adds complexity and cost

controls.name: Synthetic media and companion safety controls
controls.description: Use age assurance, content filters, provenance, and escalation paths for consumer-facing companion or media-generation products.
controls.implementation: Add child-safety review, moderation queues, rate limits, provenance metadata, and incident response for abuse reports.
controls.evidenceRequired: Safety policy, moderation logs, age-gating test results, abuse-response SLAs
controls.testingMethod: Red-team scripts and abuse-case walkthroughs
controls.limitations: Detection is imperfect and abuse tactics evolve quickly

controls.name: Unified AI Inventory and Owner Register
controls.risk: ADM transparency and privacy non-compliance; governance drift; shadow AI
controls.description: Maintain a single inventory of AI systems, agents, automated-decision workflows, and vendor dependencies across business, privacy, cyber, and procurement.
controls.implementation: Use a shared register with named owners, risk tiering, decision impact, data types, vendor links, and renewal dates.
controls.testingMethod: Reconcile the register against procurement, security, and privacy records
controls.limitations: Only works if business teams actually disclose use cases and vendors.

controls.name: Agent Tool-Use Sandboxing and Approval Gate
controls.risk: Agentic AI control and safety risk
controls.description: Prevent autonomous systems from taking externally visible or destructive actions without explicit approval and constrained credentials.
controls.implementation: Place agents in restricted environments, separate tool credentials, require human approval for sensitive actions, and log every action.
controls.testingMethod: Run abuse cases covering prompt injection, tool chaining, and privilege escalation
controls.limitations: Can reduce utility if approval paths are too slow.

controls.name: Deployment-Like Evaluation and Replay Testing
controls.description: Simulate production-like conversations and workflows before release to estimate undesired behaviors, evaluation awareness, and agentic failure modes.
controls.implementation: Replay representative histories in a privacy-preserving way, compare with prior production behavior, and tie release gates to observed deltas.
controls.testingMethod: Compare simulated-deployment results against post-release telemetry and spot-check misbehavior rates
controls.limitations: Requires representative data and careful privacy handling.

controls.name: Companion-Bot Safety and Abuse Response
controls.risk: Companion-bot child safety and harm risk
controls.description: Add age assurance, moderation, escalation, and content provenance controls to consumer AI products that could be accessed by minors.
controls.implementation: Use content filters, sensitive-topic blocking, abuse reporting, monitoring, and rapid takedown/response processes for unsafe interactions.
controls.testingMethod: Simulate minor-facing abuse cases and verify response times and escalations
controls.limitations: False positives and user friction can be significant.

controls.name: Agent least-privilege sandboxing
controls.description: Restrict every tool-using agent to the minimum permissions needed, run it in a sandbox, and require approval for destructive or externally visible actions.
controls.implementation: Use dedicated service accounts, tool allow-lists, write-once audit logs, and gated execution for deletion, payment, publishing, or production changes.
controls.evidenceRequired: Access matrix, test logs, approval records, and red-team findings
controls.testingMethod: Privilege review and adversarial tool-use testing
controls.limitations: Does not stop social engineering or approved-but-bad requests; still requires human judgment.

controls.name: ADM register and disclosure mapping
controls.description: Keep a live inventory of ADM workflows, map each to privacy-policy disclosure obligations, and identify which decisions can significantly affect rights or interests.
controls.implementation: Create a workflow register, classify impact levels, link each workflow to a privacy-policy entry, and review policy language before the December 2026 commencement.
controls.evidenceRequired: ADM inventory, policy diff, review sign-off, and decision-flow diagrams
controls.testingMethod: Quarterly control review and sample-based workflow trace tests
controls.limitations: Good inventory hygiene does not by itself guarantee lawful disclosure wording or fair outcomes.

controls.name: AI-accelerated phishing and fraud simulation
controls.description: Exercise phishing-resistant MFA, fraud-detection, and response playbooks against AI-generated lures and impersonation attempts.
controls.implementation: Run controlled simulations using text, voice, and workflow impersonation; tune controls; and rehearse incident escalation with business owners.
controls.evidenceRequired: Simulation results, MTTD/MTTR improvements, and board-level reporting
controls.testingMethod: Table-top drills and purple-team exercises
controls.limitations: Simulations improve readiness but cannot eliminate novel adversary tradecraft.

controls.name: Child-safety and synthetic-media response playbook
controls.risk: Companion Bot Harm and Synthetic Media Abuse
controls.description: Define moderation, age-assurance, escalation, and takedown steps for products that can generate intimate, sexual, or manipulative content.
controls.implementation: Add escalation paths, abuse-report tooling, event logging, and rapid containment procedures; verify that vendors can pause risky features quickly.
controls.evidenceRequired: Abuse reports, moderation metrics, response-time logs, and vendor escalation proof
controls.testingMethod: Scenario testing with synthetic-media abuse cases
controls.limitations: Effectiveness depends on enforcement, user reporting, and the reliability of age-assurance methods.

regulations.title: EU Digital Omnibus on AI (AI Act simplification)
regulations.status: Provisional political agreement (7 May 2026); pending formal Council and Parliament adoption
regulations.summary: First set of amendments to the EU AI Act since adoption in June 2024. Postpones high-risk AI system obligations (Annex III from 2 August 2026 to 2 December 2027; Annex I from 2 August 2027 to 2 August 2028). Postpones Article 50(2) synthetic-content transparency obligations from 2 August 2026 to 2 December 2026. Postpones national regulatory sandbox obligations from 2 August 2026 to 2 August 2027. Realigns AI Act with sectoral product safety rules; moves Machinery Regulation from Annex I Section A to Section B, shifting from dual-compliance to sectoral primacy. Adds new prohibited practices. Part of broader 'Omnibus VII' simplification package.

regulations.title: CMA Conduct Requirements on Google Search — AI publisher opt-out (3 June 2026)
regulations.summary: UK Competition and Markets Authority imposed new conduct requirements on Google Search under the UK's digital markets regime. Google must give UK publishers the ability to opt out of content being used in AI Overviews, AI Mode, AI model training, fine-tuning, and AI grounding without losing conventional search ranking. The CMA called this a 'world-first' requirement; Google began testing the controls the same day and said it plans to roll them out globally.
obligations: Provide publisher opt-out for AI Overviews, AI Mode, grounding, training and fine-tuning
Cite publisher content in AI-generated search with clear links
Apply controls to all changes to AI search features
regulations.timeline: Issued 3 June 2026; nine-month compliance window per the CMA; Google began testing the same day
regulations.enforcementRisk: High for non-compliance — binding conduct requirement under UK digital markets regime; enforcement can include fines and functional separation
regulations.australiaRelevance: High — Australian publishers benefit; the ACCC's digital platforms regime and News Media Bargaining Code are the Australian analogues; expected to inform ACCC and Treasury policy on AI training data and content licensing

regulations.title: APRA supervisory expectations on AI risk management and governance
regulations.summary: APRA says governance, risk management, assurance, and operational resilience are not keeping pace with AI adoption and warns against overreliance on vendor presentations. The letter frames AI as a prudential and resilience issue, not just an innovation topic.
regulations.timeline: Letter and follow-up debrief published 30 April 2026.
regulations.enforcementRisk: High

Treat cyber resilience as a licensing obligation

Align fitness and propriety to a more demanding risk environment
